content: EC2 t3 instances are burstable performance instances that accumulate CPU credits at a baseline rate. A t3.micro earns 12 CPU credits/hour with 2 vCPUs and 1 GiB RAM, suitable for low-traffic web servers and dev environments.

EC2 c6i instances are compute-optimized with 3rd Gen Intel Xeon processors, delivering up to 15% better price-performance than c5. C6i.xlarge provides 4 vCPUs and 8 GiB RAM, ideal for batch processing, HPC, and ML inference.

EC2 m6i instances are general-purpose with balanced compute, memory, and networking. M6i.large offers 2 vCPUs and 8 GiB RAM. The m6 family supports up to 128 vCPUs and 512 GiB RAM for large workloads.

EC2 r6i instances are memory-optimized for in-memory databases and real-time analytics. R6i.xlarge provides 4 vCPUs and 32 GiB RAM (8 GiB per vCPU ratio). The g5 GPU instances use NVIDIA A10G Tensor Core GPUs with up to 8 GPUs and 192 GiB GPU memory per instance.

S3 Standard storage costs $0.023/GB/month for the first 50 TB with 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability. S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between frequent and infrequent access tiers, saving up to 40% on storage costs.

S3 Infrequent Access (IA) costs $0.0125/GB/month but charges $0.01/GB retrieval fee, suitable for data accessed less than once per month. S3 Glacier Deep Archive is the cheapest at $0.00099/GB/month with 12-48 hour retrieval time.

S3 lifecycle policies automate transitions between storage classes. A typical policy: Standard for 30 days, then IA for 60 days, then Glacier for 365 days, then delete. Lifecycle rules can filter by prefix and tags, processing up to millions of objects.

RDS Multi-AZ deployments maintain a synchronous standby replica in a different Availability Zone with automatic failover in 60-120 seconds. Read replicas support up to 5 replicas per primary and use asynchronous replication with typical lag under 1 second.

Amazon Aurora delivers up to 5x the throughput of standard MySQL and 3x the throughput of standard PostgreSQL. Aurora storage auto-scales from 10 GiB to 128 TiB in 10 GiB increments with 6-way replication across 3 AZs.

Lambda cold starts range from 100-500ms for Python/Node.js runtimes and 1-10 seconds for Java/.NET. Provisioned Concurrency eliminates cold starts by pre-initializing execution environments. Maximum execution timeout is 15 minutes with up to 10,240 MB (10 GiB) memory allocation.

Lambda pricing is $0.20 per 1M requests plus $0.0000166667 per GB-second of compute. A function with 512 MB running for 200ms costs approximately $0.0000016667 per invocation. Lambda@Edge runs at CloudFront edge locations with a 5-second timeout for viewer events.

DynamoDB partition key design should aim for high cardinality to distribute data evenly across partitions. Each partition handles up to 3,000 RCU and 1,000 WCU. Hot partitions occur when a single key receives disproportionate traffic, mitigated by adding random suffixes or composite keys.

DynamoDB Global Secondary Indexes (GSI) allow querying on non-key attributes with their own provisioned throughput, supporting up to 20 GSIs per table. Local Secondary Indexes (LSI) share the base table partition key but allow alternate sort keys, limited to 5 per table and must be created at table creation.

DynamoDB on-demand mode charges $1.25 per million write request units and $0.25 per million read request units with zero capacity planning. Provisioned mode is 5-7x cheaper for predictable workloads but requires setting WCU/RCU. Auto-scaling adjusts provisioned capacity within defined min/max bounds.

SQS Standard queues deliver at-least-once with best-effort ordering, supporting nearly unlimited throughput. SQS FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and strict ordering at up to 3,000 messages/second with batching (300 without). Maximum message size is 256 KB; use Extended Client Library for up to 2 GB via S3.

SQS visibility timeout default is 30 seconds (max 12 hours). Dead letter queues capture messages that fail processing after a configurable maxReceiveCount (typically 3-5). Long polling with WaitTimeSeconds=20 reduces empty responses and API costs by up to 90%.

SNS supports pub/sub messaging with fanout to SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email, and SMS subscribers. A single SNS topic can have up to 12,500,000 subscriptions. SNS+SQS fanout pattern enables parallel processing where one published message triggers multiple independent consumers.

CloudFront has 450+ edge locations across 90+ cities in 49 countries. Default TTL is 86,400 seconds (24 hours). Cache hit ratio should target 90%+ by using consistent query string ordering and Cache-Control headers with appropriate max-age values.

CloudFront Origin Shield adds a centralized caching layer between edge locations and origin, reducing origin load by up to 50%. Lambda@Edge runs at 13 regional edge caches while CloudFront Functions run at 450+ edge locations with sub-millisecond startup and 1/6th the cost.

ECS with Fargate eliminates EC2 instance management by running containers serverlessly. Fargate pricing is per-vCPU-hour ($0.04048) and per-GB-hour ($0.004445). ECS on EC2 offers more control and is 30-50% cheaper for sustained workloads using Reserved Instances.

EKS charges $0.10/hour ($73/month) per cluster control plane plus worker node costs. EKS supports managed node groups with auto-scaling, Fargate profiles for serverless pods, and Karpenter for intelligent bin-packing that reduces compute costs by 20-40%.

IAM policy evaluation logic: explicit Deny always wins over Allow. Evaluation order is Organizations SCPs, then resource-based policies, then identity-based policies, then permissions boundaries, then session policies. The principle of least privilege mandates granting only the minimum permissions required.

IAM roles are preferred over IAM users for EC2/Lambda/ECS workloads because they provide temporary credentials that auto-rotate. Instance profiles attach roles to EC2 instances. Cross-account access uses sts:AssumeRole with external ID to prevent the confused deputy problem.

AWS Well-Architected Framework has 6 pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. Each pillar has design principles and best practices. The framework recommends multi-AZ deployments for 99.99% availability (52.6 minutes downtime/year).

AWS cost optimization strategies include: Reserved Instances (30-72% savings for 1-3 year terms), Savings Plans (up to 72% on compute), Spot Instances (60-90% savings with 2-minute interruption notice), and right-sizing with AWS Compute Optimizer recommendations based on 14-day CloudWatch metrics.
